angle.headline: Why Your AI Pilot Died in a Process Graveyard
angle.thesis: Mid-market companies are deploying AI tools to fix efficiency problems, but the tools fail because the broken processes underneath—unclear decision rights, manual handoffs, information silos—were never redesigned first.
angle.targetPersona: US-LATAM corridor business owners who have experimented with AI tools but seen limited ROI or adoption stall after the pilot phase
angle.relevanceToAudience: The US-LATAM corridor operator is under pressure to 'do more with less' and sees AI as the answer, but without fixing the operating model first—who decides what, how work flows between functions, how information moves—the technology becomes expensive shelfware. This angle names the specific sequence error: technology before operating model.

justification.angleChoice: This is the most compelling angle because it names a lived dysfunction the ICP recognizes immediately: they tried AI, it didn't stick, and they blamed the tool or the vendor. The deeper truth—surfaced across three sources (06eb789d, ec9cf524, 219288ed)—is that the operating model was never ready. Small businesses are adopting AI (06eb789d reports majority usage last year, some calling it a 'lifeline'), but they're also hitting unexpected costs and adoption failures. Administrative assistants (ec9cf524) are using AI successfully by redesigning their own workflows first (no longer taking notes manually, automating meeting summaries), while those who don't adapt face displacement. A newsletter founder (219288ed) turned a side hustle into a multimillion-dollar business by systematizing content production. The pattern is consistent: technology works when the process is clear; it fails when the process is assumed. This angle is superior to a generic 'AI adoption' trend piece or a 'small business efficiency' listicle because it names the specific sequence error (technology before model) and connects it to the Business Transformation OS framework. Other potential angles (T-MEC uncertainty, billionaire tax, sports investment) either lack direct US-LATAM corridor relevance or don't connect to a systemic operating model dysfunction.
justification.osPillarChoice: This is Operating Model OS, not Strategy OS or Technology OS, because the dysfunction is in how the business executes, not in what it decides (Strategy) or which tools it uses (Technology). The sources show that the AI tools themselves work—small businesses report efficiency gains (06eb789d), administrative assistants successfully automate tasks (ec9cf524), content creators scale production (219288ed)—but only when the underlying process is redesigned first. The operating model—decision rights, handoffs, information flow—is the layer that must be fixed before technology can deliver value. This is the classic 'broken before you bought it' pattern: the business cannot scale because the owner is in every decision, processes are undocumented and tribal, and cross-functional handoffs rely on heroics. Deploying AI into this environment doesn't fix the model; it automates the chaos. Strategy OS would address whether AI aligns with the company's competitive positioning. Technology OS would address which AI vendor or architecture to choose. But the dysfunction here is that the model—who does what, when, and how—was never designed to support the technology.
justification.peopleAngleChoice: The 'challenge' maps directly to the thesis: leadership must admit the real reason the AI pilot failed. This is not a technology problem; it's a model problem. The discomfort is in naming the truth—that the company's processes were never documented, owned, or designed for scale—and accepting responsibility for the gap. The ADKAR 'Awareness' step activates the concrete action: the owner must pull the operating data that shows where AI adoption stalled (which teams didn't use it, where manual workarounds persisted, where information got stuck) and use that data to surface the pattern. Only when the leadership team sees the cost of the current model in their own numbers will they have the desire to redesign it. This is not a training problem or a change-management communications problem; it's a model-design problem. The framework action is: show the team the evidence of the broken model first, then build the desire to fix it.

peopleAngle.challenge: The leadership team must admit that the AI tool didn't fail because it was bad technology—it failed because they deployed it into a process no one had documented, owned, or designed for cross-functional handoffs.
peopleAngle.framework: ADKAR: Awareness — The owner must first surface the cost of undocumented processes in their own operating data (where AI adoption stalled, where manual workarounds persisted) before the team will have the desire to redesign the model.

insightClaims.claim: A majority of small businesses used AI last year, with some owners calling it a lifeline and stating it's the only reason the company is still in business.

Small businesses adopting AI pay a median of about $21 per employee, compared with $11 across businesses of all sizes, and some owners reported accidentally running up big bills or fears of price increases.

Administrative assistants who adapted to AI by redesigning workflows—such as setting up Copilot and ChatGPT to take meeting notes automatically—are staying ahead, while employment projections show a grim outlook for the profession overall.
One administrative professional stated that adapting and staying ahead of the curve is key to the constantly-changing role, saying 'All you do is have to evolve.'
A newsletter founder turned a Substack side hustle into a multimillion-dollar business, with income from writing and podcasting eclipsing his CFO salary.

fieldReportAnchor.company: Deanna Danger (administrative professional using AI to redesign workflows)

enContent.subject: The reason your AI pilot stopped working in month three
enContent.preheader: The technology worked. The undocumented process didn't.
subjectOptions: Your AI tool didn't fail. Your process did.
The reason your AI pilot stopped working in month three
Small business AI spend just hit a hidden ceiling

sections.heading: The Signal
sections.body:
*This week: AI is entering companies faster than the processes it is supposed to run.*

- **Strategy:** Mark Bertolini sold Aetna to CVS for $69 billion in 2018 and later co-led Bridgewater with $168 billion in assets. He now leads Oscar Health. **Strategy at scale is a career-long apprenticeship, not a quarterly plan.** [Read in Fast Company ->](https://www.fastcompany.com/91567403/oscar-healths-mark-bertolini-on-making-healthcare-work-for-all)
- **Operating Models:** Small businesses now pay a median of $21 per employee for AI, nearly double the $11 median across companies of all sizes. **The tool is cheap. The undocumented process it lands in is what makes it expensive.** [Read in Business Insider ->](https://www.businessinsider.com/business-insider-newsletter-small-business-ai-uses-risks-2026-7)
- **Technology:** Aspire Space's CEO says an engine that would have taken seven years and up to half a billion dollars was drafted in six months through an AI collaboration. **AI collapses timelines only where the craft has already been codified.** [Read in Fast Company ->](https://www.fastcompany.com/91563020/aspire-rocket-design-spacex)
- **Human Capital:** Administrative assistants who adopted ChatGPT and Copilot early are outrunning peers whose roles are shrinking. **The people documenting their workflow are the ones surviving automation.** [Read in Fast Company ->](https://www.fastcompany.com/91569559/outlook-grim-admin-assistant-jobs-workers-using-ai-get-ahead)

sections.heading: The Apertura
sections.body:
===OPTION_A:statistic===
Small businesses now pay a median of $21 per employee for artificial intelligence tools, nearly double the $11 median across companies of all sizes, [according to Business Insider](https://www.businessinsider.com/business-insider-newsletter-small-business-ai-uses-risks-2026-7). The number reads like a premium for early adoption. It is not. It is the cost of deploying automation into processes that were never written down. A larger company can absorb that drag. A $5M to $100M operator cannot. Every dollar spent on an AI tool that lands in an undocumented workflow buys the same result: faster confusion, cleaner-looking error, and a bill the owner did not expect at the end of the quarter.
===OPTION_B:provocation===
Most AI pilots in mid-market companies are not failing because the technology is weak. They are failing because they are being deployed into processes no one has documented, owned, or designed for handoffs between functions. Small business AI spend now runs at a median of $21 per employee, nearly double the cross-industry median, [according to Business Insider](https://www.businessinsider.com/business-insider-newsletter-small-business-ai-uses-risks-2026-7). Some owners report accidentally running up bills they did not expect. The tool is not the problem. The problem is what happens when a decision engine lands inside an operating model that has never been written down.
===OPTION_C:pattern===
Three signals in the same week point in the same direction. Small businesses now spend $21 per employee on AI, nearly double the broader median, [per Business Insider](https://www.businessinsider.com/business-insider-newsletter-small-business-ai-uses-risks-2026-7). Administrative assistants who adopted the tools three years ago are outrunning peers whose roles are shrinking, [Fast Company reports](https://www.fastcompany.com/91569559/outlook-grim-admin-assistant-jobs-workers-using-ai-get-ahead). A rocket engine that would have taken seven years is being drafted in six months through an AI collaboration, [also per Fast Company](https://www.fastcompany.com/91563020/aspire-rocket-design-spacex). The pattern is not that AI is fast. The pattern is that AI multiplies whatever operating model it lands in, including the one you never wrote down.

sections.heading: The Insight
sections.body:
You bought the AI tool. Your team used it for a month, maybe two. The pilot slowed. The workaround returned. The invoice kept arriving. Six months later, you are quietly wondering whether the technology was oversold, whether your team is behind, or whether you moved too early.

None of that is the diagnosis.

The tool did not fail because it was bad technology. It failed because it was deployed into a process no one had ever documented. No owner. No written handoff between the person who starts the work and the person who finishes it. No agreement on what "done" means. The tool did exactly what it was designed to do. It accelerated whatever workflow it landed in. And the workflow it landed in was invisible.

This is the reframe most operators miss. AI does not fix an operating model. AI amplifies one. If the process is documented, owned, and designed for handoffs, the tool compresses time. If the process lives in three people's heads, the tool multiplies the ambiguity. Then it hides that ambiguity behind a cleaner-looking output.

Operating Model OS begins with evidence the owner must confront personally. Not a consultant's finding. The owner's own data. Where did AI adoption stall inside the company? Which teams reverted to the manual workaround, and how quickly? Which handoffs still route through the owner's inbox? That evidence is the awareness the owner has to sit with before the team will have any desire to redesign the process. Without it, every redesign conversation feels theoretical. Every push to document feels like bureaucracy imposed from above.

In most mid-market firms, the tool arrives before the process map. The vendor demo is impressive. The pilot is greenlit. The rollout begins. Nowhere in that sequence does anyone write down how the work actually moves. Marketing to operations. Sales to delivery. The person who takes the call to the person who fulfills it. What typically happens next is predictable. The tool sits on top of a process no one owns. The failure gets blamed on adoption.

The action this week is small and uncomfortable. Pick the one AI tool your team stopped using or is quietly working around. Pull the data on when adoption fell off. Then map the underlying process on a single page: trigger, owner, handoff, decision, close. If any of those five slots is empty, you have found where the tool was going to fail before it was installed. Fix the process on paper first. The tool comes after.

sections.heading: The Field Report
sections.body:
Anthropic signed a 20-year lease with TeraWulf for a purpose-built AI infrastructure campus in Hawesville, Kentucky. The site is a former Century Aluminum smelter that TeraWulf acquired earlier this year. [The deal is expected to generate about $19 billion of contracted revenue over the initial term](https://www.businessinsider.com/anthropic-terawulf-data-center-lease-hawesville-kentucky-2026-7). Capacity will support roughly 400 megawatts of AI workload in a city of about 1,000 people.

For the mid-market operator, the reading of this shift is that AI infrastructure now runs on 20-year operating commitments, not quarterly software subscriptions. Our view is that the value accrues to whoever owns the operating layer beneath the compute. Power procurement. Uptime protocols. Cross-functional site management. In most mid-market firms, that operating layer is the exact discipline the owner has never written down inside their own four walls. The Kentucky deal is a reminder that the tool has always been the easy part.

sections.heading: The Tool
sections.body: **Scribe.** A workflow documentation tool that auto-generates step-by-step process guides as your team works through a task on-screen. It matters because it removes the biggest excuse for undocumented processes: the time cost of writing them down. Find it at scribehow.com.

sections.heading: The Compass
sections.body: **Watch for this week:** Count how many times someone on your team asks how a routine task gets done, and how many times they check a documented process instead. Track the ratio for five working days. The gap between those two numbers is your undocumented-process tax. It is also the exact bill your next AI investment will inherit unless the process gets written down first.

esContent.subject: Por qué su piloto de IA dejó de funcionar en el tercer mes
esContent.preheader: La tecnología funcionó. El proceso sin documentar, no.
esContent.thesis: Las empresas medianas están contratando IA para resolver problemas de eficiencia, pero las herramientas fracasan porque los procesos sobre los que aterrizan, con derechos de decisión ambiguos, entregas manuales e información aislada, nunca fueron rediseñados primero.
esContent.shareableSentence: La IA no arregla un modelo operativo: lo amplifica.

sections.heading: LA SEÑAL
sections.body:
*Esta semana: la IA entra a las empresas más rápido que los procesos que debería operar.*

- **Estrategia:** Mark Bertolini vendió Aetna a CVS por 69 mil millones de dólares en 2018 y después co-lideró Bridgewater, con 168 mil millones en activos bajo administración. Hoy encabeza Oscar Health. **La estrategia a escala se construye en décadas, no en una junta.** [Leer en Fast Company ->](https://www.fastcompany.com/91567403/oscar-healths-mark-bertolini-on-making-healthcare-work-for-all)
- **Modelos Operativos:** Las pequeñas empresas pagan hoy una mediana de 21 dólares por empleado en IA, casi el doble de los 11 dólares que gastan las compañías de todos los tamaños. **Cara sale la herramienta sin proceso.** [Leer en Business Insider ->](https://www.businessinsider.com/business-insider-newsletter-small-business-ai-uses-risks-2026-7)
- **Tecnología:** El CEO de Aspire Space asegura que un motor que habría tomado siete años y hasta 500 millones de dólares se diseñó en seis meses con IA. **Acorta plazos solo donde el oficio ya está documentado.** [Leer en Fast Company ->](https://www.fastcompany.com/91563020/aspire-rocket-design-spacex)
- **Capital Humano:** Los asistentes administrativos que adoptaron ChatGPT y Copilot temprano están superando a colegas cuyos puestos se comprimen. **Quien documenta su flujo sobrevive.** [Leer en Fast Company ->](https://www.fastcompany.com/91569559/outlook-grim-admin-assistant-jobs-workers-using-ai-get-ahead)

sections.heading: LA APERTURA
sections.body: Las pequeñas empresas pagan hoy una mediana de 21 dólares por empleado en herramientas de inteligencia artificial, casi el doble de los 11 dólares que gastan las compañías de todos los tamaños en promedio, [según Business Insider](https://www.businessinsider.com/business-insider-newsletter-small-business-ai-uses-risks-2026-7). La cifra parece una prima por adoptar temprano. No lo es. Es lo que cuesta meter automatización en procesos que nadie se molestó en documentar. Una empresa grande absorbe ese sobreprecio. Un operador de entre 5 y 100 millones de dólares no. Cada peso invertido en una herramienta que cae sobre un flujo sin documentar compra el mismo resultado: confusión más rápida, error de mejor apariencia y una factura que el dueño no vio venir al cierre del trimestre.

sections.heading: EL INSIGHT
sections.body:
Usted compró la herramienta de IA. Su equipo la usó un mes, quizás dos. El piloto empezó a perder tracción. El atajo manual regresó. La factura siguió llegando. Seis meses después, en silencio, se pregunta si la tecnología estaba sobrevaluada, si su equipo quedó rezagado o si se adelantó demasiado.

El diagnóstico no es ninguno de esos.

La herramienta no falló porque fuera mala tecnología. Falló porque cayó sobre un proceso que nadie había escrito. Sin dueño. Sin acuerdo claro entre quien inicia el trabajo y quien lo cierra. Sin definición de qué significa "listo". La herramienta hizo exactamente lo que estaba diseñada para hacer: aceleró el flujo sobre el que cayó. Y ese flujo era invisible.

El giro que a la mayoría se le escapa es este. La IA no corrige un modelo operativo. Lo amplifica. Si el proceso está documentado, tiene dueño y define sus entregas, la herramienta comprime tiempo. Si el proceso vive en la cabeza de tres personas, la herramienta multiplica la ambigüedad. Y la esconde detrás de un resultado que se ve más limpio.

El Operating Model OS arranca con una evidencia que el dueño tiene que confrontar en persona. No el hallazgo de un consultor. Los datos de la propia empresa. ¿Dónde se estancó la adopción de IA dentro de la organización? ¿Qué equipos regresaron al atajo manual y en cuánto tiempo? ¿Qué entregas siguen pasando por el correo del dueño? Esa evidencia es la conciencia que el dueño necesita sostener antes de que el equipo se muestre dispuesto a rediseñar el proceso. Sin ella, cada conversación de rediseño suena teórica. Cada empuje por documentar suena a burocracia impuesta desde arriba.

En la mayoría de las medianas empresas, la herramienta llega antes que el mapa del proceso. La demo del proveedor impresiona. El piloto se aprueba. Arranca el despliegue. En ningún punto de esa secuencia alguien escribe cómo se mueve realmente el trabajo. De marketing a operaciones. De ventas a entrega. De quien contesta la llamada a quien cumple el pedido. Lo que sigue es previsible: la herramienta se monta sobre un proceso que nadie posee, y la culpa recae sobre la adopción.

Esta semana, la acción es pequeña e incómoda. Elija la herramienta de IA que su equipo dejó de usar o está evadiendo en silencio. Saque los datos de cuándo se cayó la adopción. Y dibuje el proceso subyacente en una sola hoja: disparador, dueño, entrega, decisión, cierre. Si alguna de esas cinco casillas está vacía, ahí tiene el punto donde la herramienta iba a fracasar antes de instalarse. Corrija el proceso en papel primero. La herramienta viene después.

sections.heading: EL REPORTE DE CAMPO
sections.body:
Sección: spotlight

Anthropic firmó un contrato de arrendamiento a 20 años con TeraWulf para un campus de infraestructura de IA construido a la medida en Hawesville, Kentucky. El sitio es una antigua fundidora de aluminio de Century Aluminum que TeraWulf adquirió a inicios de este año. [Se espera que el acuerdo genere cerca de 19 mil millones de dólares en ingresos contratados durante el plazo inicial](https://www.businessinsider.com/anthropic-terawulf-data-center-lease-hawesville-kentucky-2026-7). La capacidad soportará alrededor de 400 megawatts de carga de IA, en una ciudad de mil habitantes.

Para el operador de la mediana empresa, la lectura es directa: la infraestructura de IA corre hoy sobre compromisos operativos de dos décadas, no sobre suscripciones trimestrales de software. Nuestra tesis es que el valor lo captura quien controla la capa operativa debajo del cómputo. Compra de energía. Protocolos de disponibilidad. Coordinación cruzada del sitio. En la mayoría de las medianas empresas mexicanas, esa capa operativa coincide con la disciplina que el dueño nunca plasmó dentro de sus cuatro paredes. El acuerdo de Kentucky recuerda que la herramienta siempre fue la parte fácil.

**Enfoque México.** La misma disciplina se exige del otro lado del mostrador. Un año después del caso FinCEN, la SHCP, Banxico y la CNBV endurecieron el escrutinio para autorizar nuevas licencias bancarias, incluidas fintechs y Sofipos, [según El CEO](https://elceo.com/negocios/a-un-ano-del-caso-fincen-hacienda-banxico-y-cnbv-endurecen-criterios-para-autorizar-nuevos-bancos/). La gobernadora de Banxico, Victoria Rodríguez Ceja, señaló que las inspecciones han puesto la lupa sobre el modelo de calificación de riesgo. También sobre el diseño de escenarios de monitoreo más robustos.

sections.heading: LA HERRAMIENTA
sections.body: **Scribe.** Herramienta de documentación de flujos de trabajo que genera guías paso a paso de forma automática mientras su equipo ejecuta una tarea en pantalla. Importa porque elimina la excusa más común para no documentar procesos: el tiempo que lleva escribirlos. Disponible en scribehow.com.

sections.heading: LA BRÚJULA
sections.body: **A vigilar esta semana:** cuente cuántas veces alguien de su equipo pregunta cómo se ejecuta una tarea rutinaria, y cuántas veces consulta un proceso documentado. Registre la proporción durante cinco días hábiles. La diferencia entre esos dos números es el impuesto oculto que paga hoy por procesos sin documentar. Y es la misma factura que heredará su próxima inversión en IA.

sections.heading: LA PUERTA
sections.body:
Si algo de este número resonó contigo, respóndeme - leo cada mensaje.
Si le es útil a alguien en tu red, reenvíalo - es el mayor cumplido.
Cuando estés listo para trabajar juntos directamente, así es como empezamos: [link]

assets.prompt: Hand-drawn pencil sketch illustration, editorial style, white background with expansive negative space. Center: an elegant AI circuit pattern suspended mid-air above a tangled foundation of fragmented paper scraps, broken arrows, and disconnected process boxes drawn in charcoal graphite. The AI element hovers cleanly rendered, while the foundation beneath dissolves into sketched chaos—missing connections, question marks at decision points, overlapping ownership zones. Subtle orange accent highlights one critical break in the workflow chain. Architectural metaphor: a precise tool placed on an unmapped, collapsing infrastructure. Matte finish, sophisticated business editorial aesthetic, minimal composition, strategic visual hierarchy. Small WB logo in bottom corner.

altText.en: Tangled gray pipes and corroded valves in an abandoned industrial facility with debris scattered across the floor
altText.es: Tuberías grises enredadas y válvulas corroídas en una instalación industrial abandonada con escombros en el suelo

caption.en: You can't automate a process no one designed in the first place.
caption.es: No puedes automatizar un proceso que nadie diseñó desde el principio.
